title: Stop Telling Engineers to “Use AI and Be More Productive” — Give Them This Instead
author: Skdhir
text:
Stop Telling Engineers to “Use AI and Be More Productive” — Give Them This Instead
# What if I told you your manager is wrong about AI coding tools?
I mean this:
**If your manager is pushing AI coding tools by saying “use it and be more productive”… they’re doing it wrong.**
And if you’ve been frustrated, slower, or cleaning up a mess… it’s not because you “don’t know how to prompt.”
It’s because you were given a tool… without an operating system.
— -
## The uncomfortable truth
AI coding tools are *insanely* capable.
They’re also **stateless**.
Every session starts like:
> “Hi, I’ve never met you. I don’t know your architecture. I don’t know your standards. I don’t know what we decided yesterday.”
So without structure, the AI defaults to generic patterns. Sometimes it works. Sometimes it quietly breaks your system in a way that takes hours to detect.
And in teams? It gets worse:
- everyone has their own AI sessions
- everyone is generating code in parallel
- nobody has shared conventions
- nobody knows what the AI changed in the background
That’s not “productivity.”
That’s chaos with autocomplete.
— -
## What I built instead: the **AI Agent Orchestra**
I’m a solo founder. I ship fast. And I’m not doing it by working 80-hour weeks.
I run **multiple AI coding agents in parallel** — each with:
- a defined job
- a defined scope
- a defined protocol for how they work and communicate
That system is what I call **AI Agent Orchestra**.
And I open-sourced the entire thing so you can copy/paste it into your repo:
https://github.com/skdhir/ai-agent-orchestra
It’s not “a prompt.”
It’s not “a hack.”
It’s an **operating system for AI coding**.
— -
## The core idea: treat your AI like a new hire with amnesia
A great engineer can still wreck your codebase if:
- you don’t give them context
- you don’t give them boundaries
- you don’t give them a process
- you don’t give them a way to leave notes for future-you
Your AI is the same… except it forgets *everything* between sessions.
So I made the rules explicit and **version-controlled**.
— -
## Rule #1: The Brainstorm Gate (this is the cheat code)
This is the single most impactful rule in the entire system:
**Before writing ANY code, the agent must propose 2–3 options with trade-offs and wait for approval.**
Why it matters:
- the first solution an AI produces is often *plausible*, not *correct*
- options force the AI to reason instead of autopiloting
- trade-offs expose hidden risks early
It sounds “slower.”
It’s not.
That 5-minute gate prevents the 2-hour cleanup session where you discover the AI changed 9 files it never should’ve touched.
— -
## Rule #2: Parallel agents only work if scopes don’t overlap
“Just run multiple agents!” sounds cool until:
- two agents touch the same files
- your PRs conflict
- you spend more time resolving merges than shipping
So the Orchestra is built on a simple constraint:
**Non-overlapping directory scopes.**
Example role split:
- **Fullstack agent**: server + client + SDKs
- **Web/DevRel agent**: marketing site + demos
- **DevOps agent**: infra folder only
- **Architect agent**: owns process, tracker, design docs, PR review
Each agent gets its own clone and its own role contract.
You don’t need a fancy orchestration platform.
You need boundaries.
— -
## Rule #3: Agents communicate through files, not chat
Agents can’t “talk” to each other like humans do.
So don’t pretend they can.
Instead, make communication:
- asynchronous
- persistent
- version-controlled
### Mechanism A: a tracker as the task queue
A simple `tracker.csv` (or your system of choice) becomes the source of truth.
It includes stuff like:
- ID
- priority
- status (`New`, `Ready`, `In Progress`, `Done`)
- dependencies
- PR link
- comments / checkpoints
This is how work doesn’t get lost when sessions die.
### Mechanism B: design docs as the message bus
Every non-trivial feature gets a design doc:
`docs/design/{ID}.md`
Inside that doc is an **Agent Notes** section where agents leave messages like:
- what changed
- what got merged
- what another role needs to update
- what’s blocked
This is the difference between “random AI output” and “repeatable engineering.”
### Mechanism C: the human is the router
Here’s the part nobody wants to admit:
**You (the human) are the orchestrator.**
You:
- curate priorities
- choose which agent does what
- review PRs
- resolve cross-scope dependencies
- merge + deploy
And that’s fine.
At 3–4 agents, you don’t need automation.
You need a clean workflow because the human becomes the bottleneck anyway.
— -
## Rule #4: Crash recovery is not optional
AI sessions die.
Context windows fill up.
Your laptop sleeps.
The model forgets.
If your system doesn’t assume failure, you’ll constantly redo work.
So the rule is:
- mark items `In Progress` before you start
- add checkpoints in tracker comments
- only mark `Done` after PR merged and verified
That’s how you get **zero lost work** even when sessions die mid-task.
— -
## Rule #5: Make every agent clone self-sufficient
If you run multiple clones, you quickly hit a dumb problem:
“Did I update the agent rules in all clones?”
So the repo includes a setup script that:
- clones your repo into `./agents/{role}/`
- installs a `post-merge` hook so after every `git pull`, the agent’s instructions regenerate automatically
- generates role-specific instruction files by combining shared rules + role profile
Translation:
**Pull latest → rules update automatically → no manual sync.**
That’s the kind of boring automation that makes the whole system actually usable.
— -
## Quick Start (copy/paste friendly)
If you want to try this today:
1) Copy the templates into your repo
2) Customize shared rules + role scopes
3) Run the agent setup script to create clones
4) Add `agents/` to `.gitignore`
5) Open multiple VS Code windows (one per agent clone) and run your AI tool
You now have parallel agents working with boundaries and a protocol — not chaos.
Repo: https://github.com/skdhir/ai-agent-orchestra
— -
## Who this is for (and how to use it)
### If you’re an engineer
Stop trying to “prompt harder.”
Make the AI:
- brainstorm options
- plan before touching code
- stay inside a defined scope
- write tests as a requirement, not a suggestion
- ship via PRs only
You’ll move faster and feel less insane.
### If you’re a manager/director/leader
If you want AI adoption to actually work, don’t push tools.
Push a methodology.
Because “use Copilot” without structure is like:
- giving someone a power tool
- skipping training
- and acting surprised when things break
Give your team a playbook:
- brainstorming gate
- PR + review workflow
- tracker discipline
- scope boundaries
- crash recovery protocol
That’s what scales.
— -
## My results (so far)
Running multiple agents with this system let me ship like a small team:
- parallel work streams without stepping on each other
- fewer “AI did something weird” incidents
- faster PR throughput
- less context loss
- more predictable output
This isn’t theory.
It’s what I use daily.
— -
## The ask
If you’ve been told “use AI and be faster” and it felt like a trap… you’re not crazy.
You were missing the operating system.
I open-sourced mine here:
https://github.com/skdhir/ai-agent-orchestra
Steal it.
Fork it.
Improve it.
And if you’re leading a team, please stop handing out AI tools with no methodology.
That’s not leadership.
That’s outsourcing the hard part.
